title: 2025/08/20 - MA (Part 1)
description: Wednesday 20 August 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee (Part 1) Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlements Hearing of evidence Submitter list: NZ Transport Agency Waka Kotahi Richard Forgan, Group General Manager System Leaderships Tui Gilling, Pou Matua Māori Crown Partnerships Lou Mutu, Pou Arataki, Senior Manager Māori
descriptionSummary: (Part 1). Briefing on Treaty settlement legislation and Report of the Controller and Auditor-General, How public organisations are fulfilling Treaty settlements. NZ Transport Agency Waka Kotahi

chunks.text: Um, welcome to you both. Thank you very much for coming here today. So, uh, as you know, uh, we're very interested to explore, um, the recommendations, uh, particularly around the, the audit general's, uh, report itself, and hopefully that was a good guidance for you and what we wanted to, uh, talk to you about and discuss.

Uh, today. Clearly, we're wanting to make sure that, that we as the crown, that's also the many other entities that have obligations, undertreating settlements, are, uh, doing their best to, to meet the, uh, obligations, I suppose, and, and the relationships that see going forward itself. So I thought maybe how we could start is perhaps some opening comments from yourself with regards to it.
Um, and then we can perhaps delve into some conversations, some questions as no ma Thank you. Uh, o
uh, thank you for seeing, for seeing us this morning. Turn to my colleague.
I am the ua, the national, uh, Maori manager for Maori Crown Partnerships and System leadership. So I sit, um, with Richard in that part.

Um, so we're attending here at your request, uh, to outline our approach to our treaty settlement commitments and meeting them, um, to identify, address the challenges, any challenges and issues that we have faced in implementing those commitments. And that leads quite naturally to therefore how we are responding to the auditor General's report. Uh, the one leads to the other quite, quite naturally.

Um, as a Crown entity, we are not directly part of treaty settlement obligation treaty settlement negotiations, but some settlements do lead to specific obligations on us. Um, VER tends to be a, so far that has been a minority of settlements. Um, and also we have, they tend to come to us as quite narrow and specific obligations, and that's not indicative at all of the extent and breadth of engagement that we would have with Iwi Hu group model across the motto.

Um, so I'll just talk quickly about our counter across sort of facts and figures on our settlements. Um, we have specific obligations under 17 settlements. Uh, and there are basically four categories of obligation that we have, right? So first, refusal deferred selection properties, place name changes on roads, so signage, uh, and relationship redress.

Um, 14 of the settlements contain RFR obligations, and these identify there are 79 confirmed RFR identified RFR properties and two wider areas over which there are presumed rfr for any crown land, any property within those areas. Um, according to our surplus disposal records over the last 10 years, we've disposed of actually 230 property interests across both FRS and MA protection mechanism.

Uh, we follow have to follow the Crown land disposal process, uh, managed by, uh, land Information New Zealand, and that picks up every aspect of property disposal, whether it's RFR or protection mechanism or PWA offer back, it's all through the same process. Uh, and we follow that process and that ensures effectively that when land is declared surplus, those RFR obligations are met.

Um, the key improvement for us, let's go straight there, is I said the two 30. Um, we are not actually able on our current records to distinguish between the specific properties that were RFR and which were multi protection mechanism. So the bit that we're, we are particularly focused is actually the closing the loop in the record keeping.

We follow the process, we're confident in the due diligence and everything that goes into that. Where we need to improve is maturing that kind of closing the loop with our record keeping, and then our ability to engage specifically on that. With Mano or with our, with our partner treaty partners. Um, deferred selection properties, we had just two deferred selection properties across three settlements.

Um, they lapsed times on those have passed, so they are now RFR properties and treated as such, uh, in our processes and records. Um, there was some early stage correspondence many, uh, eight or nine years ago in relation to one of those properties, uh, sorry, in relation to some of those properties from one partner.

It's unclear to us actually subsequently over the years how that actually landed and ended up. So again, this points to, uh, we need to be much more systematic in our record keeping. Um, in terms of place name changes, uh, these are things that we have pretty routinely and all the time, specifically from settlements, we had eight place name change commitments to meet.

Um, those changes are made through the pro, the standard process. The New Zealand geographic board, gazettes, the name change, uh, those name changes are routinely checked by all of our maintenance contractors, and they are rolled out into new signage replacement where there's some major name change to some large area or, or or town on a large, on a large state highway route.

We will tend to accelerate that. It'll bring it faster than the normal maintenance program would replace signage. Um, all those set, those name changes were from 1997 to 2003 settlements over 20 years ago now. Um, and in terms, so then in terms of relationship redress and relationship agreements, we have just one settlement that sort of specifically sets out that, um, on part of our due diligence as we've picked this up to me and myself in the last year, um, we've identified that that relationship hasn't been monitored effectively.

So we are now in the process of picking that up and trying to get it reestablished, understand what has gone on and where it needs to go from here. It's worth also just touching quickly on, uh, other treaties settlement commitments. So whilst we're not directly named, we are, we do participate in three holder government Accords, uh, TKI Wai Accord and Accord.

Um, none of those I think have specific requirements on us, although there is capacity to expand the scope of those accords within them, particularly Y Wire. Um, but we are actively participating in those in terms of the things that affect transport and procurement and supply chain management. Um, so that's kind of a quick counter across some facts and figures around our treaty settlements and the specific obligations.

In terms of the challenges, uh, first thing we'd probably call out is, uh, funding. We receive no operational funding commitments in relation to, to our treaty settle commitments, but everything that we have been committed to by existing settlements so far is all just absorbed within our routine business as usual budgets.

So the place name changes, we absorb that within our normal maintenance and operations budgets. The property disposal cost, the cost of running property disposals, it's just part of our normal property disposals program. So funding has not been, uh, a challenge for us in that respect. Um, what has been where, where we have identified areas of improvement, I've kind of already signaled that, um, our information and records, the processes might be happening, but we have need to close the loop and be much more systematic in how we kind of track

and keep our records up to date. Um, with the information, kind of with that information loop closing, we need to improve monitoring and evaluation processes that we have around this. A again, to systematize, our challenge is, is basically around systematizing that, um, across all current future settlements.

Um, we have all of our information as we currently understand it, into hi the, uh, uni Corty database on settlement commitments. And from next year we will be, uh, publishing in our annual report, uh, information on the meeting of our specific obligations.

Um, we currently also have a, uh, statement of performance expectation measure that measures the quality of our relationships with, uh, treaty partners across the country. Um, but we'll be picking up the much more detailed reporting, uh, on that from next year. And then kind of with it internally, we need to again clarify, there are some clarification of roles, responsibilities, and accountabilities, basically to get that systematized systematic approach across the entire agency.

Uh, and with that, uh, we have, uh, a number of staff whose entire jobs are to engage with MANA and engage with Iwi and Maori business and stuff around the, around the tu. Um, they also support other teams in the performing their obligations and in running projects and managing projects. Uh, but we need to lift the capability overall, generally kind of across the organization, uh, which is probably a narrative quite wide in Wellington.

So, um, can, just to conclude those remarks, um, the specific settlement obligations to which we are subject are relatively narrowly defined, but they, and therefore they don't actually cover the, kind of the full gamut of activity which we engage with Iwi, Maori Hapu, uh, Maori business as well.

Um, there's nothing particular around road safety, for instance, or driver license promotion, all of these kind of things. It's been quite narrowly confined around property matters and signage. Um, the majority, all of these, such as the RFIs and signage changes are managed through processes which pick up and make sure that the obligations are dealt to.

But as I said already, uh, where we do need to improve is that information recording and the kind of systematic view so that we can, we can positively confirm and affirm that we have met the obligations even though we we're still nonetheless be competent, for instance, in the, the land disposals process and that we've complied with all requirements there.

Uh, I'm happy to take any questions from the committee. And perhaps the question I could direct to you, do you feel frustrated in your ability to be able to do your job of meeting the obligations of the organization that you represent? Um, um, KDA, um, my team has been established, um, to really look at the strategy function, but one of the, um, opportunities we do have is, is, um, fixing the system for treaty settlement implementation. Um, I think what is challenging is that we have obviously gaps in our record keeping and um, uh, monitoring and evaluation, but also we need to fix those 'cause we still have settlement commitments coming through.

So from a system perspective, we do need to identify and deal with the gaps now. So yes, it is frustrating, but also in a positive light we need to deal with those gaps around record keeping, um, and, um, capability growth to ensure that those settlements coming through, um, actually have a can, we can lift our game a little bit more and how we provide advice in that, in that area, and also track that whole area, which we haven't, haven't done well, um, in the past.

So, um, hopefully that answers your Question. Uh, uh, go to Dan. So what is the time, uh, line for you actually getting on top of those ones that you're trying to catch up with? Uh, and we have a whole process underway at the moment in terms of validating the information that we do have, identifying those gaps and putting that in place. Uh, my expectation is we'll get most of that nailed within the next six months. Um, if we're back here in front of this committee, for instance, in a year, unable to say that, uh, you guys will have words and we'll deserve them.
